We've got isolation steps in the playbook, but before we dive in, can you paste that list here? I'll help team run through it

ISOLATION:

Disconnect infected server – Immediately block all traffic to and from the compromised server. Isolate it from the network and any shared systems to stop the ransomware from spreading further. This is your first and fastest containment move.

Cut off infrastructure traffic – Halt all inbound and outbound network access across the infrastructure if needed. This creates a hard boundary for the attack, buying time to investigate. It may temporarily impact services, but it’s safer than letting the malware move.

Disconnect DB & freeze backup – Stop all application-level connections to the database. Pause or disable automated backup jobs to prevent infected or encrypted data from overwriting clean backups. Protecting clean copies is critical for recovery.

Detach from shared resources – If the infected server is connected to shared storage, NAS(Network attached storage), or databases, detach it immediately. Ransomware often tries to encrypt anything it can access, so this step prevents collateral damage.

Take snapshot (disk & memory) – Before making any changes, capture a complete disk image and memory snapshot. This is essential for forensic investigation, legal purposes, and possible decryption. Once altered, evidence can’t be recovered.

Kill suspicious processes – After you’ve taken snapshots, terminate the ransomware or malicious processes. This stops further encryption or damage, but killing processes too early can trigger irreversible encryption — timing matters.



RESTORATION:

Validate backups – Check all saved copies of your data to make sure they’re not infected by the attack. Only use backups from before the attack started.

Wipe infected systems – Completely erase (format) any computers or servers that were hit by the attack so no harmful files remain.

Rebuild from clean backup – Use the safe backup you found earlier to put the system back the way it was before the attack.

Re-enable security tools on servers – Before putting the systems back on the network, reinstall antivirus, firewalls, and other security software so they’re protected.

Reconnect traffic to services and monitor – Turn the systems back on, let users connect again, and keep a close eye for anything suspicious.
